title: Hello, World
lesson:
Almost every programmer's first program prints 'Hello, World', and on the LC-3 it's the classic first lab. Output works through TRAP service routines: small built-in helpers you call by name. PUTS prints a zero-terminated string whose starting address is in R0. OUT prints the single character in R0. HALT, which you've used all along, is a TRAP too.

Two new pieces make it work. LEA ('Load Effective Address') puts the address of a label into a register, which is perfect for pointing R0 at your text. And .STRINGZ lays a string into memory as a run of character codes followed by a zero terminator (that's the 'Z').

This is the same pattern as the PDP-8's Teletype, but packaged as reusable system services, the direct ancestor of the operating-system calls every program uses today to do input and output.
prompt: Print HELLO to the console. Use LEA to point R0 at a .STRINGZ message, then call PUTS, then HALT.
starterCode:
.ORIG x3000
        LEA R0, MSG    ; R0 = the address of the string
        PUTS           ; print the zero-terminated string at R0
        HALT
MSG     .STRINGZ "HELLO"
.END
hints: LEA R0, MSG loads the address of MSG into R0 (it does not load the characters themselves).
PUTS prints the string starting at the address in R0, stopping at the zero terminator.
.STRINGZ "HELLO" stores the characters plus a terminating zero.
reference:
.ORIG x3000
LEA R0, MSG
PUTS
HALT
MSG .STRINGZ "HELLO"
.END
